blocks.x:
There is a smoothie stand at the edge of the market where the owner never measures out a full batch ahead of time. She does not decide on Monday that she will make exactly twelve cups. She just keeps the blend tasting the way she likes it, and the way she does that is by holding one comparison steady. For every three chunks of mango she drops in, she adds two scoops of yogurt. Three mango, two yogurt, again and again, and the flavor lands the same whether she is whipping up a single cup or filling a giant pitcher for a crowd.

Notice what actually stays fixed here. It is not the number of mango chunks, because the pitcher clearly swallows far more mango than one cup does. It is not the number of yogurt scoops either. What holds firm from cup to pitcher is the balance between the two, the steady idea that mango and yogurt always march together in the same lockstep. That balance is the thing we are going to study, and it has a name. We call it a ratio. A ratio is a way of comparing two quantities by saying how much of one goes with each amount of the other.

That little phrase for every is the whole heart of it. Three mango chunks for every two yogurt scoops. The ratio does not pin down a total, it pins down a relationship, and a relationship can stretch to fit any size you like.

This same comparison can be written three ways, and you will meet all three. You can write it with a colon as \(3:2\). You can spell it out in words as three to two. Or you can write it as a fraction, \(\tfrac{3}{2}\), since a ratio behaves a great deal like the fractions you reduced back in Chapter 4. Of the three, the colon form \(3:2\) is the one we will reach for most often, so let it feel familiar.

By the end of this lesson you will see exactly why a single ratio can describe a tiny batch and a huge batch equally well, how to boil a ratio down to its simplest form the same way you reduced fractions, and how to take a bare ratio and turn it back into real, countable amounts you could actually scoop into a blender.

At a trail snack stand, the scoop is the same every single time. One scoop drops exactly five almonds and three dried cherries into the bag, then the next scoop does the very same thing, and so on. This is the for every five almonds there are three cherries idea made real, one scoop at a time. A customer asks for four scoops poured into a single bag. Each scoop carries its own three cherries along with it, so four scoops means four groups of three cherries riding in together. How many dried cherries are in the bag? Type the single number.
hints: Every scoop is identical, and the cherries from one scoop do not depend on the almonds at all. Just count the cherries one scoop brings, then ask how many scoops there are.
blocks.sol: The almonds are along for the ride here. What matters is that every scoop is identical, so the cherries simply repeat, three of them at a time. One scoop brings \(3\) cherries. Four scoops bring four copies of that same group, which is \(4\) groups of \(3\). So the count is \(4 \times 3 = 12\). Notice we never had to touch the almonds to answer this, the cherry side of the pairing carries its own steady count. The bag holds \(\boxed{12}\) dried cherries.

blocks.x: At a kite festival the steady rule is that for every \(3\) yellow kites in the sky there are \(5\) red ones, and the colors always rise together in that same comparison. Right now one cluster has \(9\) yellow kites up. Notice that the yellow count went from \(3\) up to \(9\), which is exactly \(3\) groups of the for every unit. Since the comparison stays fixed, the reds climb by the same number of groups. How many red kites are in that cluster? Type the single number.
hints: The phrase for every 3 yellow there are 5 red is a ratio of \(3:5\). The yellow part grew from \(3\) to \(9\). How many times bigger is that? That same multiplier has to act on the red part too, or the comparison would change.
To go from \(3\) yellow to \(9\) yellow you multiply by \(3\), since \(3\times 3 = 9\). Apply the very same \(\times 3\) to the \(5\) reds.
blocks.sol: The rule for every \(3\) yellow there are \(5\) red is the ratio \(3:5\), and a ratio works much like a fraction in simplest form. As long as you scale both parts by the same number, the comparison stays exactly the same. The yellow part has grown from \(3\) to \(9\). To find the multiplier, ask what \(3\) was multiplied by to reach \(9\). Since \(3\times 3 = 9\), the multiplier is \(3\), which means there are \(3\) groups of the for every unit. Now apply that same \(\times 3\) to the red part, because both colors rise together. That gives \(5\times 3 = 15\) red kites. You can sanity check it. The scaled comparison \(9:15\) reduces back to \(3:5\), so the ratio held steady. \(\boxed{15}\)

blocks.x: A ratio is a way of comparing two amounts by their relative size, telling you how many of one thing go with each helping of the other. When you say the comparison is \(3:2\), you are saying for every \(3\) of the first thing there are \(2\) of the second, and that pairing holds steady no matter how big the actual batch grows. Notice what a ratio does not do, on its own it never tells you how many there are altogether, since \(3:2\) could mean \(3\) and \(2\) or \(30\) and \(20\). You can write the same comparison three ways, as \(3:2\) with a colon, as "3 to 2" in words, or as the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{2}\), and the colon form shows up most often. The order carries meaning too, so mango to yogurt is a different comparison than yogurt to mango.

blocks.cap: Each group repeats the exact same pairing, three mango sitting above two yogurt. Line up one group or ten, the blend never changes, so the comparison stays \(3:2\). We can say it in words as three to two or write it as the fraction \(\tfrac{3}{2}\), but most often a ratio is written with a colon as \(3:2\).

blocks.x: A community garden plants basil and mint together in raised beds, always in the same comparison of four basil plants for every five mint plants. The lovely thing about a ratio is that it hides the actual totals. The garden could hold four basil and five mint, or eight and ten, or twelve and fifteen, the very same comparison riding on top of many different totals. Picture one full repeat of the for every unit as a little bundle of \(4\) basil and \(5\) mint, which is \(4+5=9\) plants. Any honest total has to be a whole number of these nine-plant bundles, nothing left over. This season the garden holds somewhere between \(30\) and \(40\) plants in all. What is the only possible total in that range? Type the single number.
hints: One full repeat of the comparison is \(4\) basil and \(5\) mint, so each bundle is \(4+5=9\) plants. The real total has to be made of whole bundles, so it must be a multiple of \(9\).
List the multiples of \(9\) and find the one that lands between \(30\) and \(40\). You have \(9, 18, 27, 36, 45\). Which single one fits?
blocks.sol: Each full repeat of the ratio brings \(4\) basil and \(5\) mint, a bundle of \(4+5=9\) plants. Since the garden is built from whole bundles, the total number of plants must be a multiple of \(9\). The multiples of \(9\) are \(9, 18, 27, 36, 45,\) and so on. The only one that sits between \(30\) and \(40\) is \(36\), which is exactly \(4\) bundles. Check it. Four bundles give \(4\times 4=16\) basil and \(4\times 5=20\) mint, and \(16+20=36\), still in the comparison \(4:5\). So the only possible total is \(\boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: Here is the surprising part. One ratio fits a whole family of totals, not just one. If the comparison says that for every \(a\) of the first thing there are \(b\) of the second, the real counts might be \(a\) and \(b\), or twice that, or any matching scale-up, so the ratio alone never tells you how many things there are in all. What it does pin down is the size of one full repeat, which is \(a+b\), and that is why the grand total always lands on a whole number of those repeats. To turn a ratio into actual amounts you need one more fact, either the total or one of the two amounts.

Back in Chapter 4 you learned that \(\tfrac{2}{3}\), \(\tfrac{4}{6}\), and \(\tfrac{6}{9}\) are all the same fraction. Multiplying the top and bottom by the same number never changes the value, it just dresses the fraction up in bigger numbers. A ratio behaves in exactly the same way. The comparison \(2:3\) stays the same comparison even when both sides grow, and you can check any ratio by scaling it back down to its simplest form, just like reducing a fraction. A robotics club mixes a cooling fluid that should always compare water to coolant in the ratio \(2:3\). Six different members each wrote down the amounts they used, in milliliters of water to milliliters of coolant: \(4:6\), \(8:10\), \(6:9\), \(9:12\), \(10:15\), and \(14:21\). How many of these six ratios are really the same comparison as \(2:3\)?
hints: Test each ratio by dividing both numbers by their greatest common factor, the same move you used to reduce a fraction to lowest terms. Then see whether what is left is \(2:3\).

Try \(4:6\). Both numbers divide by \(2\), giving \(2:3\), so that one matches. Now do the same simplifying step to \(8:10\) and notice it becomes \(4:5\), which is not \(2:3\). Work through all six and keep a running count of the ones that land on \(2:3\).
blocks.sol: Reduce each ratio to its simplest form and compare it with \(2:3\). For \(4:6\), both numbers divide by \(2\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(8:10\), both divide by \(2\), giving \(4:5\). Not a match. For \(6:9\), both divide by \(3\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(9:12\), both divide by \(3\), giving \(3:4\). Not a match. For \(10:15\), both divide by \(5\), giving \(2:3\). Match. For \(14:21\), both divide by \(7\), giving \(2:3\). Match. The ratios \(4:6\), \(6:9\), \(10:15\), and \(14:21\) all reduce to \(2:3\), while \(8:10\) and \(9:12\) do not. That gives \(\boxed{4}\) matching ratios.

blocks.x: A muralist mixes a soft seafoam color by combining paint in the comparison six parts teal to ten parts cream, written \(6:10\). For the main wall she needs a much larger batch, but the color has to come out exactly the same, so the two parts must grow together. She measures out enough teal for eighteen parts. Since six parts of teal grew into eighteen parts, the whole comparison has to grow the same way to keep the shade true. How many parts cream does the big batch use? Type the single number.
hints: Find what the teal was multiplied by. Six parts became eighteen parts, so ask yourself six times what equals eighteen. Whatever that factor is, the cream has to be multiplied by the very same factor.
The teal tripled, since \(6 \times 3 = 18\). To keep the ratio equivalent, scale the cream by that same \(3\). So work out \(10 \times 3\).
blocks.sol: An equivalent ratio comes from multiplying both parts by the same number, just like building an equal fraction in simplest form. First find that number by looking at the part you already know. The teal went from \(6\) parts to \(18\) parts, and \(6 \times 3 = 18\), so the multiplier is \(3\). Now apply the same \(3\) to the cream so the color stays identical. That gives \(10 \times 3 = 30\) parts of cream. As a check, the big batch is \(18:30\), and dividing both parts by \(6\) brings it back to \(3:5\), the same lowest-terms shade as \(6:10\). So the big batch uses \(\boxed{30}\) parts cream.

blocks.x: A ratio acts just like a fraction, so if you multiply both of its parts by the same number you get an equivalent ratio, the very same comparison wearing bigger numbers, the way \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{6}{8}\) are the same amount. Running that idea backward, dividing both parts by a number they share also keeps the comparison the same, and dividing by the largest factor they share, as far as it will go, writes the ratio in simplest form, which is exactly the simplest form idea from Chapter 4. A ratio is in simplest form when its two parts have no common factor larger than \(1\).

A new board game ships in a box with two kinds of tokens, red ones and blue ones, mixed together in the ratio \(18:24\). That comparison is correct, but it is bulkier than it needs to be, the same way \(\tfrac{18}{24}\) is a bulkier way of writing a simpler fraction. Back in Chapter 4 you cleaned up a fraction by dividing the top and bottom by the biggest number that went into both. A ratio reduces in exactly the same way, by dividing both parts by their greatest common factor. Find the largest number that divides both \(18\) and \(24\), divide it out of each part, and confirm nothing larger than \(1\) still divides both. What is the ratio of red tokens to blue tokens written in lowest terms?
hints: List the factors that \(18\) and \(24\) share. Both are even, so \(2\) works, and both are divisible by \(3\), so \(6\) divides each. Is there anything bigger than \(6\) that divides both?

The greatest common factor is \(6\). Divide each part by \(6\): \(18\div 6\) gives the new first number and \(24\div 6\) gives the new second number. Then check the two results share no common factor larger than \(1\).
blocks.sol: Treat the ratio like a fraction you want in simplest form. First find the greatest common factor of the two parts. The factors of \(18\) are \(1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18\), and the factors of \(24\) are \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\). The largest number in both lists is \(6\), so the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part by \(6\). For the red tokens, \(18\div 6 = 3\). For the blue tokens, \(24\div 6 = 4\). That turns \(18:24\) into \(3:4\). Finally, check that you are truly finished. The only common factor of \(3\) and \(4\) is \(1\), so nothing larger divides both and the ratio cannot shrink any further. The simplest form of the comparison is \(\boxed{3:4}\).

blocks.x: A school library keeps track of how its borrowed books come back. For every batch returned on time there is a batch that comes back late, and over one busy month the totals landed in the ratio \(45:75\), on time to late. Just like a fraction, this ratio is easier to read once you strip out everything the two numbers share. Reduce \(45:75\) to simplest form. Write your answer as a:b.
hints: Simplifying a ratio works the same way as simplifying a fraction. You want the greatest common factor of \(45\) and \(75\), the biggest number that divides both cleanly. Both end in a \(5\) or a \(0\), so \(5\) divides each, but check whether something larger does too.

Both numbers are multiples of \(15\), since \(45 = 3 \times 15\) and \(75 = 5 \times 15\). Divide each part of the ratio by \(15\), then check that the two results have no common factor left bigger than \(1\).
blocks.sol: The trick is to find the greatest common factor of \(45\) and \(75\), the largest number that divides both with nothing left over. It is tempting to stop at \(5\) since both numbers end in a \(5\), but you can do better. List what fits into each. The number \(45\) breaks into \(3 \times 3 \times 5\), and \(75\) breaks into \(3 \times 5 \times 5\). They share one \(3\) and one \(5\), so their greatest common factor is \(3 \times 5 = 15\). Now divide both parts of the ratio by \(15\). On time becomes \(45 \div 15 = 3\), and late becomes \(75 \div 15 = 5\). That gives \(3:5\). Finally, confirm it is fully reduced. The only number that divides both \(3\) and \(5\) is \(1\), so there is nothing left to cancel. The library's month came down to a clean \(\boxed{3:5}\), three on time for every five late.

blocks.cap: Multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number slides you up and down one family of equivalent ratios, so \(3:4\), \(6:8\), and \(18:24\) all name the same comparison. Climbing up means scaling both parts, and climbing down means splitting them. Dividing both parts of \(18:24\) by their greatest common factor \(6\) brings you all the way down to \(3:4\), the simplest form, where the two parts share no common factor larger than \(1\).

blocks.x: A small bakery makes a gluten-free blend by stirring together \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of a cup of almond flour and \(\tfrac{4}{9}\) of a cup of tapioca starch. The baker wants to record the ratio of almond flour to tapioca starch, but \(\tfrac{2}{3}:\tfrac{4}{9}\) is awkward to read. A ratio is cleanest when both parts are whole numbers, so think about scaling both parts up by the same amount to clear the fractions, then reducing. Simplify the ratio of almond flour to tapioca starch and write it in the form \(a:b\) in lowest terms.
hints: Both fractions sit over a denominator that divides into \(9\). Multiply each part of the ratio by \(9\). Multiplying both parts by the same number gives an equivalent ratio, the same way \(\tfrac{6}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{3}{2}\) name the same fraction.
After scaling you get \(6:4\). Now finish like you would simplify a fraction. Divide both parts by the largest number that goes into each.
blocks.sol: Start with the ratio of almond flour to tapioca starch, which is \(\tfrac{2}{3}:\tfrac{4}{9}\). Fractions make a ratio hard to read, so clear them first. Multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same number gives an equivalent ratio, and the number \(9\) works for both denominators. $$\tfrac{2}{3}\times 9 = 6 \qquad \tfrac{4}{9}\times 9 = 4$$ So \(\tfrac{2}{3}:\tfrac{4}{9}\) is the same comparison as \(6:4\). Now reduce, exactly like putting a fraction in simplest form. Both parts share a common factor of \(2\), so divide each part by \(2\). $$6\div 2 = 3 \qquad 4\div 2 = 2$$ That leaves \(3:2\), and since \(3\) and \(2\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), it is fully reduced. For every \(3\) parts of almond flour the blend has \(2\) parts of tapioca starch. \(\boxed{3:2}\)

blocks.x: A muralist mixes a custom green by comparing \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) of a liter of base paint to \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) of a liter of tint. She wants the comparison written as a clean ratio of whole numbers, the ratio of \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) to \(\tfrac{5}{6}\). A ratio with fractions in it is awkward to read, so the trick is to scale both parts up by the same number until the fractions disappear. The smallest number that clears both \(\tfrac{3}{4}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) is the least common multiple of \(4\) and \(6\), which is \(12\). Multiply both parts by \(12\), then reduce if there is anything left to reduce. What is the ratio of base to tint in lowest terms, written as \(a:b\)?
hints: Scaling both parts of a ratio by the same number never changes the comparison, it only changes how it looks. Multiply each part by \(12\). For the base, \(\tfrac{3}{4}\times 12\) is the same as \(3\times 3\). For the tint, \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times 12\) is the same as \(5\times 2\).

After clearing you should have \(9:10\). Now check whether the two whole numbers share a common factor bigger than \(1\). The factors of \(9\) are \(1, 3, 9\) and the factors of \(10\) are \(1, 2, 5, 10\), so the only factor they share is \(1\). That means it is already in lowest terms, no further reducing needed.
blocks.sol: Start from the ratio of base to tint, \(\tfrac{3}{4}:\tfrac{5}{6}\). To turn the fractions into whole numbers, multiply both parts by the least common multiple of the denominators \(4\) and \(6\), which is \(12\). Scaling both parts by the same number keeps the comparison identical, it just trades the fractions for whole numbers. The base part becomes $$\tfrac{3}{4}\times 12 = 3\times 3 = 9,$$ and the tint part becomes $$\tfrac{5}{6}\times 12 = 5\times 2 = 10.$$ So the cleared ratio is \(9:10\). The last step is always to check for a common factor. Here \(9 = 3\times 3\) and \(10 = 2\times 5\) share no factor larger than \(1\), so clearing the fractions landed us straight in lowest terms with nothing left to reduce. The ratio of base to tint is \(\boxed{9:10}\).

blocks.x: Here is the trick for a ratio that shows up wearing a fraction or a decimal. First clear the mess by scaling both parts up to whole numbers, multiplying through by a common denominator if you see fractions or by a power of ten if you see decimals. That move is safe because stretching both parts by the same factor never changes what they are comparing, exactly like the equivalent fractions you built back in Chapter 4. Then divide both whole numbers by their greatest common factor to land in lowest terms. Clear, then reduce.

A trail map shows two connecting legs of a hiking loop. The first leg runs \(0.75\) kilometers and the second runs \(1.25\) kilometers, so the map writes their comparison as the ratio \(0.75:1.25\). A ratio with decimals is awkward to read, and you can always trade it for a cleaner whole-number ratio that means exactly the same thing. Both lengths run to the hundredths place, so multiplying each part by \(100\) clears the decimals at once, and scaling both parts by the same number never changes the comparison. Clear the decimals, then reduce the result to lowest terms. What is \(0.75:1.25\) written as a whole-number ratio in lowest terms, in the form a:b?
hints: The decimals reach the hundredths place, so multiply both parts by \(100\). That turns \(0.75\) into \(75\) and \(1.25\) into \(125\), giving the whole-number ratio \(75:125\). Stretching both legs by the same factor of \(100\) keeps the comparison identical, the same way \(\tfrac{75}{125}\) and \(\tfrac{0.75}{1.25}\) are equal fractions.

Now reduce \(75:125\) the way you reduced fractions in Chapter 4. The largest number that divides both \(75\) and \(125\) is \(25\). Divide each part by \(25\) and see what whole-number ratio is left.
blocks.sol: Start with the decimal ratio \(0.75:1.25\). Both numbers stop at the hundredths place, so multiplying each part by \(100\) clears the decimals. $$0.75:1.25 = (0.75\times 100):(1.25\times 100) = 75:125$$ Multiplying both parts by the same number does not change the comparison, just like scaling the top and bottom of a fraction by \(100\) leaves its value alone, so \(75:125\) describes the very same trail. Now reduce \(75:125\) to lowest terms. The greatest common factor of \(75\) and \(125\) is \(25\), so divide each part by \(25\). $$75:125 = (75\div 25):(125\div 25) = 3:5$$ Since \(3\) and \(5\) share no factor larger than \(1\), the ratio is fully reduced. The two legs compare as \(\boxed{3:5}\).

blocks.x: The shape of a screen is described by a ratio of its width to its height, so when someone says a screen is \(16:9\) they are telling you its proportions and nothing about its actual size. Older television sets and computer monitors were nearly square at \(4:3\), while most modern TVs, laptops, and phones turned sideways are wider at \(16:9\). It is easy to check that \(16:9\) really is the wider shape. Rewrite \(4:3\) as \(16:12\) by scaling both numbers up by \(4\), and now both screens are \(16\) wide, but the old one stands \(12\) tall and the new one only \(9\), so the \(16:9\) screen is the shorter, wider rectangle. The lovely part is that one ratio describes both a tiny phone clip and a giant cinema wall, the same shape stretched across wildly different totals. Both \(4:3\) and \(16:9\) are already in lowest terms, since neither pair shares a common factor bigger than \(1\).

Turn it into a count. So far a ratio has been a kind of balance and a name, and you have learned to scale it up, reduce it down, and tidy it into lowest terms. None of that has told you how many of anything is actually there. The move that unlocks real amounts is to read a ratio as a recipe built from equal parts, \(a\) parts of one thing and \(b\) parts of the other, every part exactly the same size. Once you figure out what a single part is worth, every quantity in the story drops out by simple multiplication. The next problems build that idea twice over, first starting from a total, then starting from one quantity you already know.

Here is a fresh way to use a ratio. A snack stand fills a bag with exactly \(56\) pieces of mixed dried fruit, and the bag always holds raisins and pumpkin seeds in the ratio \(5:3\). Picture that ratio as five equal scoops of raisins sitting next to three equal scoops of seeds. Every scoop is the same size, and together all of those scoops have to make up the whole \(56\) piece bag. Figure out how big one scoop is, then use it to answer the question. How many pumpkin seeds are in the bag?
hints: The ratio \(5:3\) means the bag is built from \(5\) equal parts of raisins and \(3\) equal parts of seeds. Add those up to see how many equal parts make the whole bag.
There are \(5+3=8\) equal parts in all, and they must total \(56\) pieces. So one part is \(56\div 8\). The seeds are the \(3\) parts, so multiply that one part value by \(3\).
blocks.sol: The ratio \(5:3\) tells you the bag is made of \(5\) equal parts of raisins and \(3\) equal parts of pumpkin seeds. Notice that the ratio by itself does not tell you the totals, it only tells you how the pieces share out. To bring in the real count, add the parts. There are \(5+3=8\) equal parts, and all \(8\) of them together must add up to the whole bag of \(56\) pieces. So each part is worth $$56 \div 8 = 7 \text{ pieces.}$$ Now scale each side by that value. The raisins are \(5\) parts, which is \(5\times 7 = 35\) pieces, and the pumpkin seeds are \(3\) parts, which is \(3\times 7 = 21\) pieces. As a quick check, \(35+21=56\), exactly the size of the bag, and \(35:21\) reduces back to \(5:3\). The bag holds \(\boxed{21}\) pumpkin seeds.

blocks.x: Here is the trick that unlocks every ratio. Read \(a:b\) as a pile of equal parts, \(a\) parts of one thing sitting next to \(b\) parts of the other, which means there are \(a+b\) parts in all. If you know the total, split it evenly by dividing by \(a+b\) to find what one single part is worth, then hand \(a\) of those parts to one side and \(b\) to the other to get the real amounts. If instead you already know one of the two quantities, find the multiplier that grows its part count up to that amount, then stretch the other side by the exact same multiplier. Once you know what one part is, the whole comparison falls open.

blocks.cap: A ratio is really just a count of equal parts. The \(5:3\) ratio cuts the snack mix into \(5+3=8\) equal parts, and since those parts have to add up to the whole batch, each part is worth \(56\div 8=7\) pieces. Once you know one part, you scale each side to its real amount, \(5\times 7=35\) raisins and \(3\times 7=21\) seeds, and sure enough \(35+21=56\). This is the parts method, find the value of one part, then hand it to both sides of the ratio.

blocks.x: A printmaker mixes her ink in the ratio \(7:4\), seven parts blue to four parts white. For one poster she pours \(28\) milliliters of blue. This time you are not told the total, only one side of the mix, so the first thing to find is the multiplier, the number that turns the \(7\) blue parts into the real \(28\) milliliters. Once you know that multiplier, the same one scales the white side. How many milliliters of white ink does the mix use?
hints: You know the blue parts and the real blue amount, so find the multiplier first. What do you multiply \(7\) by to get \(28\)?
The multiplier is \(4\), since \(7\times 4=28\). The same multiplier scales the white side, so multiply the \(4\) white parts by \(4\).
blocks.sol: Here you know one quantity, the blue ink, but not the total, so start by finding the multiplier that links the parts to the real amounts. The ratio says \(7\) blue parts, and the real blue ink is \(28\) milliliters, so the multiplier is the number with \(7\times m=28\), which gives \(m=4\). Every part in this mix is worth \(4\) milliliters. The white side is \(4\) parts, so the real white ink is \(4\times 4=16\) milliliters. You can sanity check it. Blue is \(28\) and white is \(16\), and \(28:16\) reduces by dividing both by \(4\) back to \(7:4\), the ratio we started with. So the mix uses \(\boxed{16}\) milliliters of white ink.

blocks.x: A craftsperson is laying a mosaic strip out of \(64\) small tiles in two colors, gold and slate, in the ratio \(3:5\) of gold to slate. Think of the strip as being split into equal-size parts, with three of them gold and five of them slate. The slate color owns the larger share, since five parts is more than three. How many slate tiles are in that larger piece? Type the single number.
hints: The ratio \(3:5\) means the strip is built from \(3+5=8\) equal parts in all. Since the whole strip is \(64\) tiles, first find how many tiles make up just one part.
One part is \(64\div 8=8\) tiles. Slate is the larger share at \(5\) parts, so multiply the size of one part by \(5\).
blocks.sol: The ratio \(3:5\) tells you how the strip is divided, not the totals on its own, so start by counting the parts. There are \(3+5=8\) equal parts altogether. The whole strip is \(64\) tiles, so each single part is $$64\div 8 = 8 \text{ tiles}.$$ Now use the part the question actually asks about. Slate is the larger share at \(5\) parts, and each part is \(8\) tiles, so $$5\times 8 = 40 \text{ slate tiles}.$$ As a quick check, gold takes \(3\) parts, which is \(3\times 8=24\) tiles, and \(40+24=64\), matching the whole strip. The larger piece holds \(\boxed{40}\) slate tiles.

blocks.x: A box of \(84\) collectible stickers is split between two friends in the ratio \(5:7\), with the bigger share going to whoever chipped in more money. Think of the box as cut into equal-sized parts. There are \(5 + 7 = 12\) parts in all, and each part is the same number of stickers. The friend who paid more gets the \(7\)-part side, and the other friend gets the smaller \(5\)-part side. How many stickers are in the smaller share?
hints: Add the two numbers in the ratio to find how many equal parts the box is divided into. Then figure out how many stickers sit in a single part.
One part is \(84 \div 12 = 7\) stickers. The smaller share is the \(5\)-part side, so it holds five of those parts.
blocks.sol: The ratio \(5:7\) tells you the box is shared in \(5 + 7 = 12\) equal parts. To find the size of one part, divide the total by the number of parts, so \(84 \div 12 = 7\) stickers per part. The smaller share is the \(5\)-part side, which is \(5 \times 7 = 35\) stickers. As a quick check, the larger share is \(7 \times 7 = 49\), and \(35 + 49 = 84\), the whole box. The smaller share holds \(\boxed{35}\) stickers.

blocks.x: A homemade lemonade recipe always keeps lemon juice and water in the ratio \(2:9\). For one fresh batch you pour in six ounces of lemon juice, then add the right amount of water to match the recipe. The ratio tells you the shape of the mixture but not the totals, so you have to scale it up to fit your six ounces. How many ounces does the whole finished batch hold?
hints: The lemon side of the ratio is \(2\) parts, and those \(2\) parts landed on six ounces. What number do you multiply \(2\) by to get \(6\)? That single number is the multiplier for every part of the ratio.
One full repeat of the recipe is \(2 + 9 = 11\) parts. Multiply those \(11\) parts by the multiplier you found, and you have the whole batch in ounces.
blocks.sol: Start from the side you actually know. The lemon juice is \(2\) parts in the ratio, and in this batch those \(2\) parts come out to six ounces. So each single part must be worth \(6 \div 2 = 3\) ounces. That number \(3\) is the multiplier, the amount one part is really worth. Now scale the whole recipe by it. One complete repeat of the ratio is \(2 + 9 = 11\) parts, so the finished batch is \(11 \times 3 = 33\) ounces. You can check it by parts. The lemon juice is \(2 \times 3 = 6\) ounces, the water is \(9 \times 3 = 27\) ounces, and \(6 + 27 = 33\) ounces, which matches. The whole batch is \(\boxed{33}\) ounces.

blocks.x: An aquarium opens with \(40\) fish, blue and green mixed in the ratio \(3:2\). Picture that mix as parts. The blue is \(3\) parts and the green is \(2\) parts, so the tank is split into \(3+2=5\) equal parts. That makes one part \(40\div 5=8\) fish, giving \(24\) blue and \(16\) green. Now the keeper adds only green fish, never any blue, until the comparison of blue to green reads \(6:5\). The blue count is frozen at \(24\) the whole time. How many green fish must the keeper add to reach a blue to green comparison of \(6:5\)?
hints: The blue total never moves, it stays at \(24\). So your job is to find how much green pairs with \(24\) blue when the comparison is \(6:5\), then compare that to the \(16\) green already swimming there.

In the new comparison \(6:5\), the blue is \(6\) parts and equals \(24\) fish, so one part is \(24\div 6=4\). The green is \(5\) parts. Find the green count, then subtract the \(16\) you started with.
blocks.sol: Start by turning the opening ratio into real counts. The ratio \(3:2\) means \(3+2=5\) parts, and \(40\div 5=8\) fish per part, so there are \(3\times 8=24\) blue and \(2\times 8=16\) green. Only green fish get added, so the blue stays anchored at \(24\) forever. Now read the target comparison \(6:5\) the same way. Blue is the \(6\) side and it equals \(24\) fish, so one part is \(24\div 6=4\) fish. The green side is \(5\) parts, which is \(5\times 4=20\) green fish. The tank already holds \(16\) green, so the keeper needs \(20-16=4\) more. Check it, with \(24\) blue and \(20\) green the comparison is \(24:20\), and dividing both by \(4\) gives \(6:5\), exactly the goal. So the keeper must add \(\boxed{4}\) green fish.

review.x: A craft fair vendor packs identical gift bags, and every bag gets the same pairing inside, four glitter stickers and six mini erasers. She fills five bags for a school order. Since each bag holds six mini erasers, how many mini erasers does she use across all five bags? Type the single number.
hints: The ratio inside one bag pairs the stickers and erasers, but here you only need the eraser part of it. Each bag holds the same number of mini erasers, so figure out how many bags share that count.
Take the number of erasers in one bag and add it once for every bag she fills. That is five copies of six.
review.sol: A ratio inside one bag pairs the stickers and erasers, but this question asks only about the erasers, so the sticker count is a distraction. Each bag holds the same six mini erasers, and she fills five identical bags. Five bags each carrying six erasers gives \(5 \times 6 = 30\) erasers in all. So she uses \(\boxed{30}\) mini erasers across the five bags.

review.x: A swim club keeps the same staffing comparison at every session, two coaches on deck for every nine swimmers in the water. This morning thirty six swimmers showed up. You can read the swimmers as having grown from \(9\) up to \(36\), which is four repeats of the for every unit. How many coaches are on deck? Type the single number.
hints: The comparison is \(2:9\). Ask yourself how many of those nine swimmer units fit inside \(36\) swimmers.
Since \(36 = 9 \times 4\), the unit repeats four times, so the coaches repeat four times too. Multiply \(2\) by that same \(4\).
review.sol: The staffing is fixed at \(2:9\), two coaches for every nine swimmers. To keep the comparison the same when more people show up, both parts of the ratio scale by the same multiplier. The swimmers went from \(9\) to \(36\), and \(36 \div 9 = 4\), so the for every unit repeats four times. The coaches must repeat four times as well, which gives \(2 \times 4 = 8\) coaches. As a check, \(8:36\) reduces by dividing both parts by \(4\), and \(8 \div 4 = 2\) with \(36 \div 4 = 9\), so \(8:36\) is the same comparison as \(2:9\). There are \(\boxed{8}\) coaches on deck.

review.x: A flower stand bundles tulips and daffodils in the ratio \(5:6\), so every complete repeat of that comparison uses \(5+6=11\) stems. Right now the stand holds some whole number of stems that lands somewhere between \(70\) and \(80\). Because each honest total has to be a whole number of these \(11\)-stem repeats, only one total in that range can actually work. What is that total? Type the single number.
hints: A working total must split cleanly into groups of \(11\) stems, so it has to be a multiple of \(11\). Which multiples of \(11\) sit between \(70\) and \(80\)?
List the multiples of \(11\) near that range. \(11\times 6=66\) is too small and \(11\times 8=88\) is too big, so look at \(11\times 7\).
review.sol: Each full repeat of the \(5:6\) comparison uses \(5+6=11\) stems, so any real total must be made of complete groups of \(11\). That means the total has to be a multiple of \(11\). Checking the multiples of \(11\) near the range, \(11\times 6=66\) is below \(70\) and \(11\times 8=88\) is above \(80\), while \(11\times 7=77\) lands neatly between them. So the only total that splits evenly into \(5:6\) bundles here is \(77\), which gives \(7\) repeats, meaning \(35\) tulips and \(42\) daffodils. The single possible total is \(\boxed{77}\).

review.x: A fabric designer prints a repeating border where wide stripes and narrow stripes appear in the ratio \(9:12\). You can reduce that comparison in your head if you like, then scale it up so the wide stripe count climbs to \(24\). Since multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same factor keeps the comparison exactly the same, the narrow stripes scale right along with the wide ones. How many narrow stripes go with \(24\) wide ones? Type the single number.
hints: Reduce \(9:12\) first by dividing both parts by their common factor. Both \(9\) and \(12\) share a factor of \(3\), so the comparison becomes \(3:4\).
From \(3\) wide stripes to \(24\) wide stripes you multiplied by \(8\), since \(3\times 8 = 24\). Multiply the narrow part by that same \(8\).
review.sol:
Start by reducing the comparison. Both parts of \(9:12\) share a common factor of \(3\), so dividing each part gives \(\tfrac{9}{3}:\tfrac{12}{3}\), which is \(3:4\). That tells you the simplest pattern is \(3\) wide stripes for every \(4\) narrow ones.

Now scale up to reach \(24\) wide stripes. To turn the \(3\) wide stripes into \(24\), multiply by \(8\), since \(3\times 8 = 24\). To keep the comparison the same, multiply the narrow part by that very same factor, so \(4\times 8 = 32\).

You can check it against the original numbers too. Going from \(9\) wide to \(24\) wide is a factor of \(\tfrac{24}{9} = \tfrac{8}{3}\), and \(12\times \tfrac{8}{3} = 32\) as well. Either path lands in the same place. So \(24\) wide stripes go with \(\boxed{32}\) narrow stripes.

review.x: A small office juice bar tracks how often it refills two flavors. One morning it restocks mango pods and guava pods in the ratio \(24:36\). Simplify that ratio to lowest terms by dividing both parts by their greatest common factor, and make sure the two results share no factor larger than \(1\). Write your answer in the form a:b.
hints: Look for the greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(36\). What is the biggest number that divides evenly into both?
Both \(24\) and \(36\) are divisible by \(12\). Divide each part by \(12\) and then check that the two new numbers have no shared factor left.
review.sol: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so simplifying it works the same way you reduced fractions in Chapter 4. You divide both parts by the same number until nothing is left to cancel. The fastest route is to divide by the greatest common factor of the two parts at once. The greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(36\) is \(12\), since \(12\) divides both and no larger number does. Dividing each part by \(12\) gives \(24 \div 12 = 2\) and \(36 \div 12 = 3\), so the ratio becomes \(2:3\). Now check that it is fully reduced. The only positive factor shared by \(2\) and \(3\) is \(1\), so there is nothing left to cancel and the ratio is in lowest terms. The simplified ratio is \(\boxed{2:3}\).

review.x: A small bakery tracks its weekend baking. Over one week it sells \(42\) sourdough loaves and \(54\) rye loaves, so the comparison of sourdough to rye is \(42:54\). The owner wants to write this in the simplest form possible by dividing out the greatest common factor of \(42\) and \(54\). What is the ratio of sourdough to rye in simplest form? Give your answer in the form a:b.
hints: A ratio simplifies just like a fraction. Find the largest number that divides both \(42\) and \(54\) evenly, then divide each part by it.
Both \(42\) and \(54\) are even, and both are divisible by \(3\), so they share a factor of \(6\). Divide each side by \(6\).
review.sol: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so simplest form means the two parts share no common factor larger than \(1\). Start by finding the greatest common factor of \(42\) and \(54\). The factors they share are \(2\) and \(3\), and \(2\times 3=6\), so the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part of the ratio by \(6\). For the sourdough, \(42\div 6=7\), and for the rye, \(54\div 6=9\). That gives the simplified ratio \(7:9\). Since \(7\) and \(9\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. So the bakery sells sourdough and rye in the ratio \(\boxed{7:9}\).

review.x: A juice stand mixes a berry smoothie by comparing the amount of berries to the amount of banana. Each blend uses \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) cup of berries and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) cup of banana. The owner wants the comparison written as a clean whole-number ratio of berries to banana, with no fractions left in it. Multiply both parts by a common denominator to clear the fractions, then reduce the result to lowest terms. What is the ratio of berries to banana in simplest form, written as a:b?
hints: The two amounts are \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\). A number that both \(6\) and \(9\) divide into evenly is \(18\), so try multiplying both parts of the ratio by \(18\).
Multiplying turns \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) into \(15\) and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) into \(10\), so the ratio becomes \(15:10\). Now divide both numbers by their greatest common factor to finish.
review.sol: The two amounts being compared are \(\tfrac{5}{6}\) cup of berries and \(\tfrac{5}{9}\) cup of banana, so the ratio is \(\tfrac{5}{6}:\tfrac{5}{9}\). A ratio does not change when you multiply both parts by the same number, just like an equivalent fraction. Pick a number that clears both denominators. Both \(6\) and \(9\) divide evenly into \(18\), so multiply each part by \(18\). The berries part becomes \(\tfrac{5}{6}\times 18 = 15\), and the banana part becomes \(\tfrac{5}{9}\times 18 = 10\). That gives the whole-number ratio \(15:10\). Now reduce it. The greatest common factor of \(15\) and \(10\) is \(5\), so divide both by \(5\) to get \(15\div 5 = 3\) and \(10\div 5 = 2\). The ratio in lowest terms is \(\boxed{3:2}\).

review.x: A jar of homemade window glaze blends two thinning liquids. The recipe calls for \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup of a fast-drying liquid for every \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) cup of a slow-drying one. Right now the comparison is between two fractions, which is awkward to read. Multiply both amounts by \(8\) so they turn into whole numbers, then simplify the comparison to lowest terms. What is the ratio of the fast-drying liquid to the slow-drying liquid, written in the form a:b?
hints: Multiplying both parts of a ratio by the same number gives an equivalent ratio. Try multiplying each amount by \(8\) and see what whole numbers you get.
After scaling, you have \(4\) to \(3\). Check whether \(4\) and \(3\) share any common factor bigger than \(1\).
review.sol: The two amounts are \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup and \(\tfrac{3}{8}\) cup, so the ratio is \(\tfrac{1}{2} : \tfrac{3}{8}\). Scaling both parts of a ratio by the same number keeps the ratio equivalent, just like building up equivalent fractions. Multiply each part by \(8\). The first part becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2} \times 8 = 4\), and the second becomes \(\tfrac{3}{8} \times 8 = 3\). That turns the comparison into \(4 : 3\). Now check for common factors. The factors of \(4\) are \(1, 2, 4\) and the factors of \(3\) are \(1, 3\), so the only shared factor is \(1\). Since nothing larger divides both, \(4 : 3\) is already in lowest terms. The ratio of fast-drying to slow-drying liquid is \(\boxed{4:3}\).

review.x: A landscaper is laying out two parallel rows in a garden bed. One row measures \(0.6\) meters across and the row beside it measures \(1.5\) meters across, so the lengths compare in the ratio \(0.6:1.5\). A clean ratio should use whole numbers in lowest terms. Rewrite \(0.6:1.5\) as a ratio of whole numbers in simplest form. Write your answer in the form a:b.
hints: A ratio behaves like a fraction, so you can multiply both parts by the same number without changing what it means. Both parts have one decimal place, so think about what single multiplier turns each into a whole number.
Multiplying both parts by \(10\) gives \(6:15\). Now find the largest number that divides both \(6\) and \(15\), and divide both parts by it.
review.sol: Start with \(0.6:1.5\). A ratio works the same way a fraction does, so multiplying both parts by the same number keeps the comparison the same. Both numbers have one decimal place, so multiply both parts by \(10\) to clear the decimals. That gives \(6:15\). Now reduce to lowest terms. The largest number that divides both \(6\) and \(15\) is \(3\), so divide both parts by \(3\). You get \(6\div 3 = 2\) and \(15\div 3 = 5\), which is \(2:5\). Since \(2\) and \(5\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The rows compare in the ratio \(\boxed{2:5}\).

review.x: A soup recipe describes its base by comparing \(2.4\) cups of broth to \(0.9\) cups of cream, giving the ratio \(2.4:0.9\). A ratio reads more clearly when both parts are whole numbers in lowest terms. Multiply both parts by \(10\) to clear the decimals, then divide out the greatest common factor. Write the fully reduced ratio in the form a:b.
hints: Both parts have a digit in the tenths place, so multiply each part by \(10\). That turns \(2.4:0.9\) into a ratio of two whole numbers.
Once you have \(24:9\), find the greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(9\), then divide both parts by it.
review.sol: Start by clearing the decimals. Multiplying both parts of \(2.4:0.9\) by \(10\) keeps the comparison the same, since scaling both parts by the same amount gives an equivalent ratio. That gives \(24:9\). Now reduce to lowest terms, just like simplifying a fraction. The greatest common factor of \(24\) and \(9\) is \(3\), so divide both parts by \(3\). You get \(24\div 3 = 8\) and \(9\div 3 = 3\), which leaves \(8:3\). Since \(8\) and \(3\) share no common factor larger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The recipe holds \(8\) parts broth for every \(3\) parts cream, so the answer is \(\boxed{8:3}\).

review.x: A bracelet is strung with seventy two beads in two colors, copper and jade, in the ratio \(4:5\). That ratio hides the totals, so first turn the parts into a real bead count. The four parts of copper and the five parts of jade make \(4+5=9\) equal parts in all, and the whole bracelet of \(72\) beads is split evenly across those nine parts. How many jade beads, the five-part color, are on the bracelet? Type the single number.
hints: Add the two parts of the ratio to see how many equal parts the whole bracelet is cut into, then find what one part is worth.
One part is \(72\div 9 = 8\) beads. Jade is the five-part color, so multiply the value of one part by \(5\).
review.sol: The ratio \(4:5\) tells you copper takes \(4\) parts and jade takes \(5\) parts, so the bracelet is divided into \(4+5=9\) equal parts. Those nine parts share the \(72\) beads evenly, so one part is worth $$72\div 9 = 8 \text{ beads}.$$ Jade is the five-part color, so it gets five of those equal parts, \(5\times 8 = 40\) beads. As a quick check, copper gets \(4\times 8 = 32\) beads, and \(40+32=72\), which matches the whole bracelet. So the number of jade beads is \(\boxed{40}\).

review.x: A streaming app builds a ninety minute playlist and splits the whole thing between two genres in the ratio \(7:3\), jazz to folk. To find the value of a single part, divide the ninety minutes by the ten total parts. The larger, jazz piece is worth seven of those parts. How many minutes long is the jazz piece? Type the single number.
hints: The ratio \(7:3\) hides the total. Add the two numbers in the ratio to see how many equal parts the playlist is cut into, then share the ninety minutes among them.
First find the value of one part by dividing, \(90 \div 10 = 9\) minutes. The jazz piece is seven of those parts, so multiply \(7\) by the value of one part.
review.sol: The ratio \(7:3\) tells you the playlist is split into \(7 + 3 = 10\) equal parts, with jazz getting \(7\) of them and folk getting \(3\). Find the value of one part by sharing the ninety minutes among the ten parts, \(90 \div 10 = 9\) minutes per part. The jazz piece is seven parts, so \(7 \times 9 = 63\) minutes. As a quick check, folk gets \(3 \times 9 = 27\) minutes, and \(63 + 27 = 90\), the whole playlist. The jazz piece is \(\boxed{63}\) minutes.

review.x: A potting soil blend mixes compost and sand in the ratio \(3:8\). One batch is made by repeating that mix several times, so every repeat adds \(3\) parts compost and \(8\) parts sand. A worker pours in fifteen kilograms of compost for the batch, where one part equals one kilogram. Find the multiplier that turns the three compost parts into fifteen kilograms, then scale up the whole mix so each repeat is \(3 + 8 = 11\) kilograms. What is the total mass of the batch in kilograms? Type the single number.
hints: The compost is the 3 part of the ratio, and it weighs 15 kilograms. What number do you multiply 3 by to reach 15? That same multiplier scales every part of the mix.
One full repeat of the mix is \(3 + 8 = 11\) kilograms. Multiply that by the multiplier you found to get the whole batch.
review.sol: The ratio \(3:8\) means compost takes \(3\) parts and sand takes \(8\) parts, so one full repeat of the mix is \(3 + 8 = 11\) kilograms. The compost is the \(3\) part, and here it actually weighs \(15\) kilograms. To find the multiplier, ask what \(3\) is multiplied by to give \(15\). That is \(15 \div 3 = 5\), so the real batch is \(5\) copies of the basic mix. Now scale the whole mix by that same multiplier. One repeat weighs \(11\) kilograms, so the full batch is \(11 \times 5 = 55\) kilograms. You can check it by parts. Compost is \(3 \times 5 = 15\) kilograms and sand is \(8 \times 5 = 40\) kilograms, and \(15 + 40 = 55\) kilograms. The total mass of the batch is \(\boxed{55}\).

review.x: A reef tank holds thirty five fish, all of them either blue or gold. The blue fish and the gold fish sit in the ratio \(3:4\). First use the parts method to find how many of each color are in the tank to start. Then the keeper decides to add more blue fish, and only blue fish, leaving the gold count exactly as it is. She keeps adding until the comparison of blue to gold reaches \(1:1\), a perfectly even split. How many blue fish must she add?
hints: The ratio \(3:4\) splits the tank into \(3+4=7\) equal parts. Divide the thirty five fish among those parts to find the value of one part, then count the blue and the gold fish you begin with.
Adding only blue fish leaves the gold count frozen. A \(1:1\) split means blue must rise until it equals the gold count. Subtract the starting blue count from that target to see how many you tack on.
review.sol: The ratio \(3:4\) divides the tank into \(3+4=7\) equal parts. With \(35\) fish in all, one part is \(35\div 7=5\) fish. So the tank starts with \(3\times 5=15\) blue fish and \(4\times 5=20\) gold fish, which checks out since \(15+20=35\). Now only blue fish get added, so the gold count stays locked at \(20\). An even split of \(1:1\) means blue must climb until it matches the gold, so blue needs to reach \(20\). It began at \(15\), so the keeper adds \(20-15=5\) blue fish. \(\boxed{5}\)

blocks.x: There is a kombucha brewer who sells at a weekend market, and she never bottles a fixed amount ahead of time. Some weekends are slow and some are busy, so instead of memorizing exact cup counts she holds one comparison steady, the same trick the smoothie owner used in 7.1. For every 6 cups of brewed tea she stirs in 4 cups of fruit juice. That is the familiar two-way comparison \(6:4\), and it never told us the total, only how tea and juice line up against each other. Now she changes one thing. Every single batch, large or small, she also grates in 1 cup of fresh ginger. Suddenly three counts march together instead of two, 6 cups of tea, 4 cups of juice, 1 cup of ginger, all stepping in time. Notice that the for every idea did not break when the third ingredient showed up, it just grew a third seat at the table. We can write all three at once as \(6:4:1\), read aloud as six to four to one, and it behaves exactly like last lesson. It still fixes only the relative sizes and never pins down a total, so a tiny test jar and a giant party batch can both be \(6:4:1\). That is the whole point of this lesson. Everything you already know about two-quantity ratios, the three notations, the hidden total, equivalent ratios, simplifying, the parts method, all of it carries straight over to ratios with three or more parts. Then near the end we add one genuinely new move, snapping two separate ratios together where they share a quantity. For now, just step through the doorway.

A garden center keeps one fixed potting mix. For every 5 scoops of compost it stirs in 3 scoops of peat and 2 scoops of coarse sand, written as the ratio \(5:3:2\). Suppose you make exactly one unscaled batch, with no doubling or halving and no scaling of any kind, so you use the plain \(5:3:2\) amounts. How many scoops of potting mix is that in total?
hints: A multi-way ratio like \(5:3:2\) names the actual amount of each part in one batch. So this batch really is 5 scoops of compost, 3 scoops of peat, and 2 scoops of sand.
Every scoop ends up in the same pile, so the total is just the parts of the ratio added together. Add \(5+3+2\).
blocks.sol: A three-way ratio works the same way a two-way ratio does, it just tracks more parts at once. The ratio \(5:3:2\) tells you that one batch is 5 parts compost, 3 parts peat, and 2 parts sand. Since you are using one unscaled batch, each part is exactly 1 scoop, so the amounts are 5 scoops, 3 scoops, and 2 scoops. Everything goes into the same pile, so the total is the sum of all the parts, \(5+3+2=10\). The whole batch is \(\boxed{10}\) scoops.

blocks.x: A marching band always lines up in the same fixed pattern. The players arrive in the comparison \(5:3:1\), and the band director reads that ratio out loud as brass to woodwinds to percussion, in that exact order. A brand new member is handed only that one ratio and those three labels, nothing else, no counts and no totals. The order is doing all the work here, since each spot in the ratio is locked to the label that sits in the same spot in the reading. Reading the ratio in that order, how many parts of it belong to the woodwinds?
hints: The labels are read in the same order as the numbers. The first number goes with brass, the second number goes with woodwinds, the third number goes with percussion. So which number sits in the woodwinds spot?
Line them up. Brass matches the \(5\), woodwinds matches the \(3\), and percussion matches the \(1\). The woodwinds part is the middle number.
blocks.sol: In a multi-way ratio the order is the meaning. Each number is glued to the label in the matching position of the reading. Reading \(5:3:1\) as brass to woodwinds to percussion lines them up cleanly. Brass takes the first part, \(5\). Woodwinds takes the second part, \(3\). Percussion takes the third part, \(1\). The new member only needs the woodwinds spot, and that is the middle number, so the woodwinds share is \(\boxed{3}\) parts.

blocks.x: A ratio does not have to stop at two things. You can stand three or more quantities side by side and compare them all at once, writing it \(a:b:c\) and reading it "a to b to c", where the order is the whole point, since it tells you which number belongs to which quantity. And just like a ratio of two things, it only pins down how the amounts compare to each other, their relative sizes, and says nothing on its own about how much there is altogether. So everything you already know still holds, equivalent ratios, simplifying, the parts method, all of it. There are simply more parts now, and you keep every one of them in step.

blocks.cap: This is one batch of fizz-punch shown as a single bar cut into \(6+4+1=11\) equal parts. The bar tells you nothing about how big the cup is, only how the three drinks compare, \(6\) parts soda water to \(4\) parts cranberry juice to \(1\) part ginger syrup. You could pour tiny shots or fill a giant bowl, and as long as the parts keep the ratio \(6:4:1\) it tastes the same. The part counts are what stay fixed while the real amounts scale up together.

blocks.x: A florist builds every window arrangement in the same comparison, \(3:2:4\) for roses to lilies to ferns, always in that order. On different days the window holds a different total number of stems, but the comparison never changes. The florist swears the window could never hold exactly \(8\) stems in all. Without listing every possibility, find the smallest total number of stems that fits the \(3:2:4\) comparison exactly, using whole stems and at least one of each kind. Type the single number.
hints: The three numbers \(3\), \(2\), \(4\) tell you the size of each share inside one full set. Add them up to see how many stems a single set of shares uses, then ask whether you could ever use fewer than that and still keep at least one of each.

One full set is \(3\) roses, \(2\) lilies, and \(4\) ferns. Bigger windows just stack more identical sets, so every valid total is a multiple of that one set's size. The smallest total is the size of a single set, which is why \(8\) cannot work.
blocks.sol: A multi-way ratio hides a total, just like a two-way ratio does. The comparison \(3:2:4\) splits the window into \(3+2+4=9\) equal shares, so one full set is \(3\) roses, \(2\) lilies, and \(4\) ferns, which is \(9\) stems. The greatest common factor of \(3\), \(2\), and \(4\) is \(1\), so the comparison is already in lowest terms and you cannot shrink it any further. Every window that fits the comparison is some whole number of these sets, so every valid total is a multiple of \(9\), like \(9\), \(18\), \(27\), and so on. Since \(8\) is less than one full set, there is no way to keep at least one of each kind and still land on \(8\), which is exactly why the florist rules it out. The smallest total that works is a single full set, \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: A multi-way ratio works just like a two-way one. It locks in how the parts compare and leaves the totals wide open. When a mix follows the comparison \(a:b:c\), the real amounts could be \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\), or twice each as \(2a\), \(2b\), \(2c\), or any matched scale-up \(na\), \(nb\), \(nc\), and every single one of those is the same comparison. The trick is that the whole triple grows together by one shared multiplier, never one part on its own, which is exactly why a ratio nails down how the parts relate without ever telling you how big the whole batch is, and why one comparison can show up at all sorts of different total sizes.

A muralist mixes her signature slate-blue by stirring together \(18\) ounces of blue, \(12\) ounces of white, and \(30\) ounces of black, always kept in that same comparison. She wants to jot the recipe down in its cleanest form, the smallest whole-number version of \(18:12:30\), so anyone could scale it up later. Find the greatest common factor shared by all three parts, divide each part by it, and write the simplified ratio of blue to white to black in the form \(a:b:c\) in lowest terms.
hints: With a three-way ratio you reduce the same way you would a two-way one, except the factor has to divide every part at once. Ask what is the largest whole number that goes evenly into \(18\), into \(12\), and into \(30\) all three.

List the factors of each part and hunt for the biggest one they share. \(18\) splits as \(6\times 3\), \(12\) as \(6\times 2\), and \(30\) as \(6\times 5\), so the common factor is \(6\). Divide all three parts by \(6\).
blocks.sol: To simplify a multi-way ratio, divide every part by the greatest common factor that all the parts share. So first find the largest whole number that divides \(18\), \(12\), and \(30\) evenly. Checking the biggest candidates, \(6\) works for all three since \(18 = 6\times 3\), \(12 = 6\times 2\), and \(30 = 6\times 5\), and nothing larger divides all three. So the greatest common factor is \(6\). Now divide each part by \(6\): blue becomes \(18\div 6 = 3\), white becomes \(12\div 6 = 2\), and black becomes \(30\div 6 = 5\). That gives \(3:2:5\), and since \(3\), \(2\), and \(5\) share no common factor beyond \(1\), it is fully reduced. The cleanest form of the recipe is \(\boxed{3:2:5}\).

blocks.x: To put a many-part ratio in lowest terms, divide every single part by the greatest common factor that all the parts share together, not a factor that only fits two of them. It is the same move as reducing a fraction, just spread across more numbers at once. Watch out for the trap of staring at one small part and deciding you must be finished. A part being tiny on its own means nothing. You are done only when no whole number bigger than \(1\) divides all of the parts at the same time.

A strategy board game ships with three kinds of tokens, and the box says they come in the comparison \(6:10:15\), action tokens to coin tokens to gem tokens. A player glances at the first two numbers and notices that \(6\) and \(10\) are both even, sharing a factor of \(2\), so she figures the whole ratio must shrink down to something smaller. But watch the trap here. Two of the parts can share a factor while all three together do not. Find the greatest common factor of \(6\), \(10\), and \(15\) at once, then simplify the whole comparison. If it is already as small as it goes, the simplified ratio is just the one you started with. Write your answer in the form \(a:b:c\) in lowest terms.
hints: To simplify a multi-way ratio you cannot just look at two parts at a time. You need a number that divides into every part at once. Check each part: does \(2\) go into \(6\), \(10\), and \(15\)? Does \(3\)? Does \(5\)? A factor only counts if it works for all three.

List the factors of each part. \(6 = 2\times 3\), \(10 = 2\times 5\), \(15 = 3\times 5\). The pairs each share something, but no single number divides all three. So the greatest common factor of all three is \(1\), and dividing every part by \(1\) changes nothing.
blocks.sol: To simplify a multi-way ratio, you divide every part by the greatest common factor of all the parts at once, not just one pair. So break each number into its factors. \(6 = 2\times 3\), \(10 = 2\times 5\), and \(15 = 3\times 5\). Now look for a factor shared by all three. The number \(2\) divides \(6\) and \(10\) but not \(15\). The number \(3\) divides \(6\) and \(15\) but not \(10\). The number \(5\) divides \(10\) and \(15\) but not \(6\). Each pair shares something, yet no single number bigger than \(1\) divides all three. That makes the greatest common factor of \(6\), \(10\), and \(15\) equal to \(1\). Dividing every part by \(1\) leaves the comparison exactly as it was, so the ratio is already in lowest terms. The player's instinct was a trap. A shared factor inside a pair does not mean the whole thing shrinks. The simplified ratio is \(\boxed{6:10:15}\).

blocks.x: A baker keeps a dry blend on a card by the mixing bowl. It calls for \(1\tfrac{1}{3}\) cups of oat flour, \(2\) cups of rice flour, and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) of a cup of almond meal. Written straight off the card the comparison is \(1\tfrac{1}{3}:2:\tfrac{2}{3}\), which is awkward to read because two of the parts are fractions. A ratio reads cleanest when every part is a whole number, so scale all three parts up by the same amount to clear the fractions, then divide out any common factor. What is the simplified ratio of oat flour to rice flour to almond meal, written as a:b:c in lowest terms?
hints: Every part is either a whole number or a number of thirds, so think about what single multiplier would turn all three into whole numbers at once. Multiplying every part by \(3\) does it, because \(1\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{3}\) and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\) both clear when tripled.
After scaling by \(3\) you get \(4:6:2\). Now find the greatest common factor of all three parts and divide every part by it to reach lowest terms.
blocks.sol: First rewrite the awkward part as an improper fraction so the whole blend is in thirds. Oat flour is \(1\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{4}{3}\), rice flour is \(2=\tfrac{6}{3}\), and almond meal is \(\tfrac{2}{3}\). Every part is now a number of thirds, so multiplying all three parts by \(3\) clears the denominators at the same time. That gives \(\tfrac{4}{3}\times 3=4\), \(2\times 3=6\), and \(\tfrac{2}{3}\times 3=2\), so the ratio becomes \(4:6:2\). Scaling every part by the same number keeps the comparison the same, it just dresses it in whole numbers. Now simplify. The greatest common factor of \(4\), \(6\), and \(2\) is \(2\), so divide every part by \(2\) to get \(2:3:1\). Since \(2\), \(3\), and \(1\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The blend compares oat flour to rice flour to almond meal as \(\boxed{2:3:1}\).

blocks.x: When a many-part ratio shows up with a fraction or a mixed number hiding in any of its parts, the same clear-then-reduce move from 7.1 still works, you just run it across every part at once. First pick a number that all the bottoms divide into, a common denominator for the whole lineup, and multiply every part by it so the fractions and mixed numbers all turn into whole numbers together. Stretching every part by the same factor never changes what they are comparing, so the ratio you get is the same comparison wearing whole numbers. Then finish the usual way, dividing every part by the greatest common factor they all share to land in lowest terms.

A glass jar terrarium is built up in layers by volume, sand to gravel to potting soil, in the ratio \(3:5:7\). Filled to the top, the jar holds exactly \(60\) scoops of material. Use the parts idea here. Add up the parts to see how many equal pieces the \(60\) scoops split into, work out how many scoops one part is worth, then scale up to the layer you are asked about. How many scoops of gravel does the terrarium use? Type the single number.
hints: The ratio \(3:5:7\) means the 60 scoops are shared into \(3+5+7\) equal parts. Add those to find the total number of parts.
There are \(15\) parts in all, so one part is \(60\div 15\) scoops. Gravel is the \(5\) in the ratio, so it gets \(5\) of those parts.
blocks.sol: The ratio \(3:5:7\) tells you the jar is split into \(3+5+7=15\) equal parts. Those \(15\) parts together make up all \(60\) scoops, so one part is worth $$\frac{60}{15}=4 \text{ scoops}.$$ Gravel is the middle number in the ratio, so it takes up \(5\) parts. Five parts at \(4\) scoops each gives \(5\times 4=20\) scoops. (As a check, sand is \(3\times 4=12\) and soil is \(7\times 4=28\), and \(12+20+28=60\), the full jar.) So the gravel uses \(\boxed{20}\) scoops.

blocks.x: The parts trick does not care how many shares there are. Read a ratio like \(a:b:c\) as one big pile of equal parts, add every number to learn there are \(a+b+c\) parts in all, then divide the real total by that count to find what a single part is worth. From there each quantity is just its own part count times that one-part value, so you scale every side by the same step. It is the exact same engine from before with two, three, four, or more shares riding along, and the only thing that changes is how many numbers you add up at the start.

blocks.cap: A stained-glass panel of 84 chips split among amber, teal, clear, and rose in the ratio \(5:4:3:2\). The parts method still works with four shares. Add the parts to get \(5+4+3+2=14\), then one part is \(84\div 14=6\) chips. Scale each share to its real count, \(5\times 6=30\), \(4\times 6=24\), \(3\times 6=18\), and \(2\times 6=12\), and they rebuild the whole panel since \(30+24+18+12=84\).

blocks.x: A community choir always keeps its three sections in the same comparison, \(6:5:4\), sopranos to altos to tenors in that order. That holds at every rehearsal, no matter how big the turnout is. Tonight the room fills up and exactly 18 sopranos take their places. Think of the comparison as one batch of 6 sopranos, 5 altos, and 4 tenors repeated some number of times. The soprano part tells you how big each batch is, and once you know that, every other section follows. Using the soprano part to find what one part is worth, how many tenors are at the rehearsal tonight? Type the single number.
hints: The sopranos hold the soprano part of the comparison, which is 6. So 18 sopranos fill up 6 equal parts. Divide to find what just one part is worth, then remember the tenors hold 4 of those same parts.
One part is \(18 \div 6 = 3\) singers. Tenors take the \(4\) part, so multiply \(4 \times 3\).
blocks.sol: The comparison \(6:5:4\) splits the choir into \(6+5+4 = 15\) equal parts in all, but you only need the part size to answer this. The sopranos own the first number, so they fill \(6\) of those equal parts. Tonight 18 sopranos walk in, which means those 6 parts together hold 18 singers. One part is therefore \(18 \div 6 = 3\) singers. Every section is measured in these same parts, and the tenors own the \(4\), so the tenors hold \(4\) parts of \(3\) singers each, giving \(4 \times 3 = 12\). A quick sanity check fits the comparison nicely, since \(18\) sopranos, \(15\) altos, and \(12\) tenors reduce straight back to \(6:5:4\). So the number of tenors is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: A small orchard always plants its trees in the same fixed comparison, \(4:3:2\), apple to pear to plum, in that exact order. This season the grower walks the rows and counts exactly \(24\) plum trees. She does not know how many apple or pear trees there are yet, but she does know that every part of the ratio stands for the same number of trees. If you can figure out what one part is worth, you can build all the way up to the whole orchard. How many trees are in the whole orchard altogether? Type the single number.
hints: The plum trees are the \(2\) part of the ratio \(4:3:2\). So those \(24\) plum trees are spread evenly across \(2\) equal parts. Divide to find what just one part is worth.
One part is \(24 \div 2 = 12\) trees. Now count how many parts the whole orchard has, \(4+3+2\), and multiply that many parts by \(12\).
blocks.sol: The ratio \(4:3:2\) compares apple to pear to plum, so the plum trees sit in the \(2\) part. The grower counted \(24\) plum trees, and those \(24\) trees fill exactly \(2\) equal parts. To find the value of a single part, share the plum count across its parts, \(24 \div 2 = 12\) trees per part. Every part of this ratio is worth that same \(12\) trees. The whole orchard is built from all the parts together, \(4+3+2 = 9\) parts. Multiply the number of parts by the value of one part, \(9 \times 12 = 108\). As a check, apple is \(4 \times 12 = 48\), pear is \(3 \times 12 = 36\), and plum is \(2 \times 12 = 24\), and \(48 + 36 + 24 = 108\). So the whole orchard holds \(\boxed{108}\) trees.

blocks.x: Here is where a new kind of puzzle walks in. Every ratio so far has handed you all its parts at once, neatly lined up in a single comparison like \(5:8:2\), and all the moves from 7.1 carry straight over to those, scaling, reducing, reading off counts. But real situations are rarely that tidy. More often you get two separate comparisons that happen to overlap on one shared quantity, one ratio tying together this pair of things and a second ratio tying together that pair, with a single quantity sitting in both. You want all three things in one picture, a clean \(a:b:c\) that holds every comparison at once. The trouble is that the shared quantity almost never shows up as the same number in the two ratios, so they refuse to line up on sight. The next few problems uncover the one move that fixes exactly that and snaps the two ratios into a single one.

Here is the genuinely new move. In a bicycle gear train, the front gear turns the middle gear in the comparison \(4:3\), front to middle. The middle gear turns the rear gear in the comparison \(2:5\), middle to rear. The middle gear is shared by both comparisons, but the two ratios count it as \(3\) in one and \(2\) in the other, so they do not line up yet. Rescale each ratio so the middle number matches, snap them into a single front to middle to rear ratio, and read off the comparison of the front gear to the rear gear. Write the ratio of front gear to rear gear in the form \(a:b\) in lowest terms.
hints: The middle gear is counted as \(3\) in the first ratio and \(2\) in the second. To stitch the two ratios together you need the middle number to be the same in both. Pick a common multiple of \(3\) and \(2\), and \(6\) is the smallest one.

Scale \(4:3\) up by \(2\) to get \(8:6\), and scale \(2:5\) up by \(3\) to get \(6:15\). Now both say \(6\) for the middle, so line them up as front to middle to rear and look at just the front and rear numbers.
blocks.sol: The two ratios share the middle gear, but they count it differently. Front to middle is \(4:3\), so the middle shows up as \(3\). Middle to rear is \(2:5\), so the middle shows up as \(2\). Before I can chain them into one front to middle to rear ratio, those middle numbers have to agree. I need a common multiple of \(3\) and \(2\), and the smallest is \(6\). Scale the first ratio up so its middle becomes \(6\). Multiply \(4:3\) by \(2\) to get \(8:6\). Scale the second ratio up so its middle becomes \(6\) too. Multiply \(2:5\) by \(3\) to get \(6:15\). Now both ratios call the middle gear \(6\), so they snap together into one chain, \(8:6:15\), reading front to middle to rear. The front gear is \(8\) and the rear gear is \(15\). Their comparison is \(8:15\), and since \(8\) and \(15\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), it is already in lowest terms. So the front gear to rear gear ratio is \(\boxed{8:15}\).

blocks.x: When one ratio tells you \(A:B\) and another tells you \(B:C\), they both talk about the same middle quantity \(B\), but they almost never print the same number for it, so the two ratios sit there looking like strangers. The trick is to make that shared part match. Scale each ratio up until both show the same number for \(B\), usually a common multiple of the two \(B\) values, and the moment those numbers line up the three quantities click together into one longer ratio \(A:B:C\). The shared quantity is the hinge, and once everything hangs on it you can read off any comparison you want, even \(A:C\), which neither ratio ever showed you directly.
