blocks.cap: Two ratios that share a quantity, \(4:3\) for front to middle and \(2:5\) for middle to rear, do not line up while their shared part is written as \(3\) in one and \(2\) in the other. Scaling the first by \(2\) and the second by \(3\) turns both middles into \(6\), so the bars snap together into \(8:6:15\) and you can read off \(8:15\) for front to rear. Matching the shared part is the whole trick that lets two ratios join into one.

blocks.x: A paint studio mixes a custom shade in two stages. The first recipe compares yellow to blue as \(3:2\). A second recipe compares blue to white as \(4:7\). Blue is the color both recipes share, but it shows up as \(2\) in the first and as \(4\) in the second, so the two recipes are not speaking the same language yet. Rescale one recipe so the blue numbers agree, then line all three colors up as yellow to blue to white. What is the ratio of yellow to white, written as \(a:b\) in lowest terms?
hints: Blue is \(2\) in the first ratio and \(4\) in the second. To make them match, double everything in the first ratio so its blue becomes \(4\). What does \(3:2\) become when you double both parts?

After doubling, yellow to blue is \(6:4\) and blue to white is \(4:7\). The blue \(4\) is now the same in both, so stack them into one chain \(6:4:7\). Read off just the yellow and the white parts, then reduce.
blocks.sol: The shared color is blue, but it carries different numbers in the two recipes, so first make those numbers agree. Blue is \(2\) in the yellow to blue ratio and \(4\) in the blue to white ratio. Doubling the first ratio turns its blue into \(4\) without changing the comparison, since multiplying both parts by the same number gives an equivalent ratio. So \(3:2\) becomes \(6:4\). The second ratio already has blue as \(4\), so leave it as \(4:7\). Now both recipes agree that blue is \(4\), and you can line all three colors up in one chain as yellow to blue to white, \(6:4:7\). Reading off only the first and last parts gives yellow to white as \(6:7\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(7\) is \(1\), so this is already in lowest terms. The ratio of yellow to white is \(\boxed{6:7}\).

blocks.x: Strike a major chord on a piano and it sounds settled, almost like it has come home, and a big reason is hiding in a ratio with three parts. The three notes of the chord, the root, the major third, and the fifth, vibrate at frequencies that line up very close to \(4:5:6\). Tucked right inside that comparison is one of the small relationships people have loved for ages, the fifth above the root, since the root and the fifth give \(4:6\), which is just \(3:2\). The octave, the other famously consonant pairing at \(2:1\), is the next building block your ear leans on, and stacking those tidy whole-number relationships is part of why the chord rings as consonant to us. The parts \(4\), \(5\), and \(6\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), so \(4:5:6\) is already in lowest terms. It is just a three-way ratio doing real work in the world, the same kind of comparison you are learning to read and simplify in this lesson.

A sourdough baker keeps her recipe in two separate notes. One note says the flour to water ratio is \(5:3\). The other note says the water to starter ratio is \(6:5\). Water is the ingredient both notes mention, but it shows up as \(3\) in the first and as \(6\) in the second, so the two notes do not yet speak the same language. Once you rescale them so the water amounts agree, the three ingredients fold into a single flour to water to starter ratio. The baker mixes one batch using exactly these comparisons, and the three ingredients together weigh \(630\) grams. How many grams of flour are in the batch?
hints: The water amount is 3 in the first ratio and 6 in the second. Rescale one ratio so both read the same number for water. Doubling \(5:3\) gives \(10:6\), which now matches the \(6\) in the water to starter ratio, so you can stitch them into one flour to water to starter ratio.
Once the three-way ratio is \(10:6:5\), add the parts to see how many equal shares the \(630\) grams splits into, find what one share weighs, then take the flour parts.
blocks.sol: The two ratios share water, but they disagree on its number. Flour to water is \(5:3\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). To link them, rescale the first ratio so its water matches the \(6\) in the second. Multiplying \(5:3\) by \(2\) gives \(10:6\), so flour to water is \(10:6\) and water to starter is \(6:5\). Now both call water \(6\), and the three line up into one ratio, \(10:6:5\). The greatest common factor of \(10\), \(6\), and \(5\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. Add the parts to get \(10+6+5=21\) equal shares. The batch weighs \(630\) grams, so one share weighs \(\tfrac{630}{21}=30\) grams. Flour is \(10\) shares, which is \(10\times 30 = 300\) grams. As a check, water is \(6\times 30 = 180\) and starter is \(5\times 30 = 150\), and \(300+180+150 = 630\). So the batch contains \(\boxed{300}\) grams of flour.

review.x: A backyard birdseed blend follows a fixed recipe. For every \(7\) parts millet there are \(4\) parts sunflower and \(2\) parts cracked corn, which we can write as the multi-way ratio \(7:4:2\) in that order. Suppose you mix exactly one full unscaled batch, meaning you use the recipe numbers just as they are written without scaling them up or down. How many parts of birdseed do you end up with in total? Type the single number.
hints: A multi-way ratio like \(7:4:2\) hides a total inside it. The whole blend is made of all of its parts added together.
Add the three part numbers, \(7\), \(4\), and \(2\). One unscaled batch means you do not multiply any of them first.
review.sol: A multi-way ratio carries a hidden total, the sum of all its parts. Here the blend is \(7\) parts millet, \(4\) parts sunflower, and \(2\) parts cracked corn. One full unscaled batch uses those numbers exactly as written, so no rescaling is needed and each part keeps its given size. Add every part to find the whole, \(7 + 4 + 2 = 13\). So one batch is \(\boxed{13}\) parts of birdseed in total.

review.x: A printmaker keeps a notebook of every ink blend she uses. One favourite blend is logged as the ratio \(20:50:35\), cyan to magenta to yellow, in drops. She wants to record it in lowest terms so the same recipe written in any size still matches. Divide every part by the greatest common factor of all three parts so the results share no factor larger than \(1\). Write the simplified ratio in the form \(a:b:c\).
hints: To simplify a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the same number, the greatest common factor of all three parts. List the factors of \(20\), \(50\), and \(35\) and find the largest one they all share.

All three parts end in \(0\) or \(5\), so they are all multiples of \(5\). Check whether anything bigger than \(5\) divides all three. It does not, since \(35\) is not even, so \(5\) is the greatest common factor. Divide each part by \(5\).
review.sol: First find the greatest common factor of all three parts. The factors of \(20\) are \(1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20\), the factors of \(50\) are \(1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50\), and the factors of \(35\) are \(1, 5, 7, 35\). The largest number on all three lists is \(5\), so the greatest common factor is \(5\). Now divide every part by \(5\). You get \(20 \div 5 = 4\), then \(50 \div 5 = 10\), then \(35 \div 5 = 7\). That gives \(4:10:7\). Check that this is fully reduced. The parts are \(4\), \(10\), and \(7\), and \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(4\) or \(10\), so the three parts share no common factor larger than \(1\). The blend in lowest terms is \(\boxed{4:10:7}\).

review.x: A small batch of homemade trail mix is built around a fixed comparison of oats, raisins, and seeds, written as the ratio \(10:9:25\). Before measuring out a giant festival batch, the maker wants the cleanest possible form of this comparison, so she checks whether all three numbers share a common factor she could divide out. Reduce \(10:9:25\) to lowest terms by dividing every part by the greatest common factor of all three numbers, and if nothing divides cleanly into all three, then the ratio you started with already is the answer. Write your answer in the form a:b:c.
hints: To reduce a multi-way ratio you need the greatest common factor of all three parts at once, not just two of them. A number only counts if it divides \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) without leaving a remainder. Notice that \(9\) is odd, so \(2\) is out, and \(9\) is not a multiple of \(5\), so \(5\) is out too.

Check the factors of \(9\), which are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Does \(3\) divide \(10\)? Does \(9\) divide \(10\)? If the only number that divides all three parts is \(1\), the ratio is already in lowest terms and you leave it exactly as it is.
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio you divide every part by the greatest common factor of all three numbers together, so the real task is to find what \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) share. Look at \(9\) first, since it is the most restrictive. It is odd, so \(2\) cannot divide it, which already rules out any even common factor. Its only factors are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). Now test \(3\) against the others. It does not divide \(10\), since \(10=3\times 3+1\), so \(3\) is not a common factor. The same goes for \(9\). That leaves only \(1\) as a number dividing all three parts, so the greatest common factor of \(10\), \(9\), and \(25\) is \(1\). When the greatest common factor is \(1\), there is nothing to divide out, and the ratio is already as small as it can get. Dividing each part by \(1\) just gives back \(10:9:25\), so the comparison was already in lowest terms from the start. \(\boxed{10:9:25}\)

review.x: A flower farmer fills a long border bed using a fixed planting comparison of marigolds to basil to thyme. In one stretch of the bed she counts \(42\) marigolds, \(14\) basil plants, and \(28\) thyme plants, written as the multi-way ratio \(42:14:28\). Put this comparison in lowest terms by dividing every part by the greatest common factor of all three parts. Write your answer in the form \(a:b:c\).
hints: A multi-way ratio is in lowest terms when the greatest common factor of all three parts is \(1\). Start by listing the factors of each part and finding the largest number that divides \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\) all at once.
Each part is even and each is a multiple of \(7\), so try dividing every part by \(14\). Keep the order the same: do \(42\div 14\), then \(14\div 14\), then \(28\div 14\).
review.sol: To reduce a multi-way ratio I divide every part by the greatest common factor of all the parts, not just two of them. The parts are \(42\), \(14\), and \(28\). The factors of \(14\) are \(1, 2, 7, 14\), and the largest of these that also divides \(42\) and \(28\) is \(14\) itself, since \(42=14\times 3\) and \(28=14\times 2\). So the greatest common factor of all three is \(14\). Now I divide each part by \(14\), keeping the order marigolds, basil, thyme. That gives \(42\div 14=3\), then \(14\div 14=1\), then \(28\div 14=2\), so the ratio becomes \(3:1:2\). I can check by scaling back up, since \(3\times 14:1\times 14:2\times 14=42:14:28\), the original. The new parts \(3\), \(1\), and \(2\) share no common factor bigger than \(1\), so this is fully reduced. \(\boxed{3:1:2}\)

review.x: A breakfast smoothie is built from \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) cup mango, \(\tfrac{1}{6}\) cup spinach, and \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) cup yogurt, written in that order as \(\tfrac{1}{3} : \tfrac{1}{6} : \tfrac{1}{2}\). Scale all three parts up by the same whole number to clear the fractions, then reduce. What is the simplified ratio of mango to spinach to yogurt in the form a:b:c?
hints: The three denominators are 3, 6, and 2. Find one number that all of them divide into evenly, then multiply every part by it so the fractions disappear.
The smallest number that 3, 6, and 2 all divide into is 6. Multiply each cup amount by 6 to get whole numbers, then check whether those whole numbers share a common factor you can divide out.
review.sol: To compare these fairly, the cup amounts need to be whole numbers. The denominators are 3, 6, and 2, and the smallest number all three divide into evenly is 6. Multiply every part by 6. Mango becomes \(\tfrac{1}{3}\times 6 = 2\), spinach becomes \(\tfrac{1}{6}\times 6 = 1\), and yogurt becomes \(\tfrac{1}{2}\times 6 = 3\). Multiplying all three parts by the same number keeps the ratio equivalent, so \(\tfrac{1}{3} : \tfrac{1}{6} : \tfrac{1}{2}\) is the same as \(2:1:3\). Now check for a common factor across all three parts. The greatest common factor of 2, 1, and 3 is 1, so the ratio is already in lowest terms. The simplified ratio of mango to spinach to yogurt is \(\boxed{2:1:3}\).

review.x: A small studio mixes a signature candle from \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\) ounces of soy wax, \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\) ounces of beeswax, and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) ounce of fragrance oil. To compare these cleanly, scale every part up by the same whole number until the fractions disappear, then divide out the common factor. Written in lowest terms as \(a:b:c\), what is the ratio of soy wax to beeswax to fragrance oil?
hints: Every part is a number of eighths. Multiply each part by 8 to clear all the fractions at once, and you get three whole numbers.
After scaling you have \(20:10:5\). Find the greatest common factor of all three parts and divide each part by it.
review.sol: Start with the three amounts \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\), \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\), and \(\tfrac{5}{8}\). The smallest denominator that fits all of them is 8, so scaling every part up by 8 will clear the fractions while keeping the ratio the same. Soy wax becomes \(2\tfrac{1}{2}\times 8 = 20\), beeswax becomes \(1\tfrac{1}{4}\times 8 = 10\), and fragrance oil becomes \(\tfrac{5}{8}\times 8 = 5\). That gives the whole-number ratio \(20:10:5\). Now divide out the common factor. The greatest common factor of \(20\), \(10\), and \(5\) is \(5\), so divide each part by \(5\) to get \(4:2:1\). Since the parts \(4\), \(2\), and \(1\) share no factor bigger than \(1\), this is fully reduced. The ratio of soy wax to beeswax to fragrance oil is \(\boxed{4:2:1}\).

review.x: A craftsperson lays a mosaic border made of \(96\) small tiles, mixing gold, copper, and slate in the comparison \(1:2:5\), gold to copper to slate, in that order. Use the parts method to find what one part is worth, then answer just this. How many copper tiles are in the border? Type the single number.
hints: Add the three numbers in the ratio to count the parts. There are \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts hidden in those \(96\) tiles, so first work out how many tiles sit in one part.
One part is \(96\div 8=12\) tiles. Copper is the middle number, so it takes up \(2\) of those parts. Multiply to finish.
review.sol: The ratio \(1:2:5\) splits the border into \(1+2+5=8\) equal parts, and all \(96\) tiles fill those \(8\) parts evenly. One part is worth $$96\div 8=12\text{ tiles}.$$ Copper matches the middle number in the ratio, which is \(2\) parts, so the copper count is \(2\times 12=24\). As a quick check, gold takes \(1\times 12=12\) tiles and slate takes \(5\times 12=60\) tiles, and \(12+24+60=96\), which matches the border. So the number of copper tiles is \(\boxed{24}\).

review.x: A jeweler strings a single \(75\)-bead bracelet so that four kinds of beads always appear in the comparison \(2:4:3:6\), reading jade to amber to onyx to pearl in that order. To split the bracelet, add the parts to see how many equal shares the \(75\) beads break into, find what one share is worth, then scale up to the kind you want. How many pearl beads are on the bracelet? Type the single number.
hints: Add all four parts of \(2:4:3:6\) to find how many equal shares the bracelet splits into, then divide \(75\) by that count to learn what a single share is worth.
One share is \(75 \div 15 = 5\) beads. Pearl carries \(6\) of those shares, so multiply \(5\) by \(6\).
review.sol: The comparison \(2:4:3:6\) tells you how the \(75\) beads divide into equal shares. Add every part to count the shares, \(2+4+3+6 = 15\), so the whole bracelet is built from \(15\) equal shares. Find what one share is worth by splitting the total evenly, \(75 \div 15 = 5\), so each single share is \(5\) beads. Now scale up to the kind the question asks about. Pearl holds \(6\) shares, so the pearl count is \(6 \times 5 = 30\). As a check, jade gives \(2 \times 5 = 10\), amber gives \(4 \times 5 = 20\), and onyx gives \(3 \times 5 = 15\), and \(10 + 20 + 15 + 30 = 75\), which matches the bracelet exactly. So the bracelet carries \(\boxed{30}\) pearl beads.

review.x: A robotics club sorts its members into three roles, builders, coders, and designers, kept in the comparison \(8:5:3\) in that order. Tonight every coder showed up and there are \(35\) coders in the room. Use the coder share to figure out what one part of the comparison is worth, then say how many builders the club has. Type the single number.
hints: The coder share is the middle number, \(5\) parts. If those \(5\) parts equal \(35\) coders, what is one part worth?
Builders are the first number, \(8\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by \(8\).
review.sol: The comparison \(8:5:3\) means builders take \(8\) parts, coders take \(5\) parts, and designers take \(3\) parts, all the same size. The coders are the link here, so start there. There are \(35\) coders sitting in \(5\) parts, so one part is worth \(35 \div 5 = 7\) members. Now rescale to builders. Builders take \(8\) parts, and each part is worth \(7\), so the club has \(8 \times 7 = 56\) builders. As a quick check, designers would be \(3 \times 7 = 21\), and \(56 + 35 + 21 = 112\) members in total, which is \(16\) equal parts of \(7\). The number of builders is \(\boxed{56}\).

review.x: A produce crate is packed in the comparison \(2:9:4\), plums to apples to pears, in that exact order. The crate holds 28 pears. Find what one part is worth from the pears, then use it to answer this. How many apples are in the crate? Type the single number.
hints: The pears sit in the last slot of \(2:9:4\), so they take 4 equal parts. If those 4 parts together make 28 pears, what is one single part worth?
Once you know one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pears, the apples take 9 of those same parts. Multiply \(9 \times 7\).
review.sol: In the comparison \(2:9:4\) the pears are the third number, so they are worth 4 parts. The crate holds 28 pears, and those 28 pears fill 4 equal parts, so one part is \(28 \div 4 = 7\) pieces of fruit. Now look at the apples. They are the middle number, worth 9 parts. Each part is 7, so the apples come to \(9 \times 7 = 63\). That gives \(\boxed{63}\).

review.x: A relay team splits its weekly training distance in the comparison \(6:5:4:3\), run to bike to swim to row, in that order. The team knows the bike leg, which is \(5\) parts of the comparison, comes to \(20\) kilometers. Work out what one part is worth, then use that to find the total. How many kilometers is the whole training distance? Type the single number.
hints: The bike leg is worth \(5\) parts and equals \(20\) kilometers. One part is \(20 \div 5\).
Add the parts \(6+5+4+3\) to see how many parts the whole distance is, then multiply that by what one part is worth.
review.sol: The comparison \(6:5:4:3\) is already in lowest terms, since the only number that divides all four parts is \(1\). The bike leg sits at the \(5\) in that comparison, so it is worth \(5\) parts, and you are told those \(5\) parts equal \(20\) kilometers. Rescale to find one part. One part is \(20 \div 5 = 4\) kilometers. The whole distance covers every part, so add them up, \(6+5+4+3 = 18\) parts. Multiply to get the total, \(18 \times 4 = 72\) kilometers. So the whole training distance is \(\boxed{72}\) kilometers.

review.x: A spice jar blends cumin, paprika, and chili in the ratio \(7:2:1\), cumin to paprika to chili in that order. The jar has 18 grams of paprika. How many grams of spice are in the jar altogether? Type the single number.
hints: Paprika sits at 2 parts in the ratio, and those 2 parts are worth 18 grams. Split that to find what a single part weighs.
Once you know one part, add up the parts. The whole jar is \(7+2+1\) parts, so multiply the value of one part by that total.
review.sol: Paprika is the middle number in \(7:2:1\), so it stands for 2 parts. Those 2 parts weigh 18 grams, so one part is \(18\div 2 = 9\) grams. The whole jar is \(7+2+1 = 10\) parts. Multiply the value of one part by the total number of parts to rescale up to the full jar, \(10\times 9 = 90\) grams. As a check, cumin is \(7\times 9 = 63\) grams, paprika is \(2\times 9 = 18\) grams, and chili is \(1\times 9 = 9\) grams, and \(63+18+9 = 90\). \(\boxed{90}\)

review.x: A clockwork toy has three gears in a row. When the first gear turns the second, their turning ratio is \(5:6\). When the second gear turns the third, their turning ratio is \(4:3\). The second gear is shared by both ratios, but it appears as \(6\) in the first one and as \(4\) in the second one. Rescale so the second-gear numbers agree, line up first to second to third, then compare just the first gear to the third gear. What is the ratio of the first gear to the third gear, written as a:b in lowest terms?
hints: The second gear is the link between both ratios. Make its number the same in each one. It shows up as \(6\) and as \(4\), so find the smallest number both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into, which is \(12\).
Scale \(5:6\) up to \(10:12\) and scale \(4:3\) up to \(12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in both, so the full chain is \(10:12:9\). Read off only the first and third numbers.
review.sol: The second gear is the shared quantity, so the first job is to make its number match in both ratios. It appears as \(6\) in \(5:6\) and as \(4\) in \(4:3\). The smallest number that both \(6\) and \(4\) divide into is \(12\). Rescale the first ratio by multiplying both parts by \(2\), which gives \(5:6 = 10:12\). Rescale the second ratio by multiplying both parts by \(3\), which gives \(4:3 = 12:9\). Now the second gear reads \(12\) in each, so the three gears line up as \(10:12:9\). The first gear is \(10\) and the third gear is \(9\), so their comparison is \(10:9\). The greatest common factor of \(10\) and \(9\) is \(1\), so it is already in lowest terms. \(\boxed{10:9}\)

review.x: A garden supply shop blends a fertilizer from two separate label comparisons. On the first label, nitrogen to phosphorus is given as \(2:3\). On the second label, phosphorus to potash is given as \(9:8\). Phosphorus is the ingredient both labels share, but it shows up as \(3\) in the first comparison and \(9\) in the second, so the two ratios do not line up yet. Rescale the first ratio so its phosphorus number matches the \(9\) in the second, stitch the two together into a single nitrogen to phosphorus to potash ratio, then read off how nitrogen compares to potash. Write the nitrogen to potash ratio in the form a:b in lowest terms.
hints: Phosphorus is the link. It reads \(3\) in the first ratio and \(9\) in the second, so multiply the whole first ratio by something to turn that \(3\) into a \(9\).
Once the phosphorus parts both equal \(9\), write one combined ratio nitrogen to phosphorus to potash, then ignore the phosphorus part and compare just nitrogen to potash.
review.sol: The two ratios share phosphorus, but they measure it with different numbers, so the first job is to make those numbers agree. In \(2:3\) the phosphorus part is \(3\), and in \(9:8\) the phosphorus part is \(9\). Multiply every part of the first ratio by \(3\) to lift that \(3\) up to \(9\). That turns nitrogen to phosphorus from \(2:3\) into \(6:9\). Now both ratios speak about phosphorus as \(9\), so they snap together into a single chain, nitrogen to phosphorus to potash equals \(6:9:8\). The question only asks how nitrogen compares to potash, so pull out those two parts, \(6:8\). The greatest common factor of \(6\) and \(8\) is \(2\), and dividing both by \(2\) gives \(\boxed{3:4}\).
